talk_title: Fundamentals of Deep Learning for Digital Pathology

speaker.name: Alex Pearson
speaker.affiliation: University of Chicago, Department of Medical Oncology and Computational Science

MOA_summary: Pearson described the architecture of convolutional neural networks and vision transformers applied to whole slide H&E images, and introduced foundation models that learn a language-like embedding from 1.5 million slides (120,000 patients). These embeddings can be fine‑tuned for downstream tasks such as mutation prediction, tumor detection, and segmentation, while highlighting bias sources (e.g., ancestry prediction from TCGA site effects) and mitigation strategies (color normalization, batch correction, synthetic image explainability).
key_findings: Trained a pathology foundation model on 1.5 million H&E slides from 120 k patients, generating a reduced‑dimensional feature vector for each image.
Demonstrated that foundation models can achieve high accuracy on tasks such as ancestry prediction, but identified spurious site‑related biases in TCGA data.
Showed that synthetic GAN‑generated images can be used to train pathologists to recognize molecular patterns (e.g., BRAF‑like vs RAS‑like) improving human prediction performance.
Presented an FDA‑cleared breakthrough‑designated AI tool for Trop2‑ADC companion diagnostics, illustrating clinical translation of digital pathology AI.

talk_title: Implementation of AI Models for Pathology: Prospective Validation and Clinical Deployment

biomarkers.name: EGFR mutation status
biomarkers.finding: Model achieved comparable PPV/NPV to rapid PCR tests and enabled a 43% reduction in rapid test utilization while preserving diagnostic accuracy

models.name: EGFR H&E prediction model
models.genotype_or_key_features: trained on MSK Impact cohort (tens of thousands of slides) with internal and external validation

clinical_data.population: lung adenocarcinoma patients undergoing molecular accession

MOA_summary: Cambiamalla presented a prospectively validated AI model that infers EGFR mutation status directly from H&E whole‑slide images, allowing the model to act as a non‑exhaustive computational biomarker; by setting high‑sensitivity or high‑specificity thresholds the workflow can safely triage patients away from rapid PCR testing, reducing unnecessary assays by 43% without loss of diagnostic performance.
key_findings: Developed an end‑to‑end EGFR prediction pipeline using a foundation model fine‑tuned on >80,000 H&E slides from MSK Impact.
Internal and external validation showed no performance drop compared with the original research cohort.
Prospective silent trial demonstrated a 43% reduction in rapid EGFR PCR tests while maintaining equivalent PPV and NPV to the gold‑standard workflow.
Independent third‑party validation confirmed robust performance across institutions.
Proposed scaling the silent‑trial framework to multi‑center deployments for additional lung cancer biomarkers and low‑resource settings.

combination_strategies.rationale: Use AI model as a triage tool to avoid unnecessary rapid PCR when model predicts negative with high confidence
combination_strategies.phenotype: 43% reduction in rapid test usage while preserving diagnostic accuracy

talk_title: AI‑Enabled Spatial Multi‑Omics: From 2D Tissue Maps to 3D Biomarkers

speaker.name: Lihua Wang
speaker.affiliation: MD Anderson Cancer Center, Department of Pathology

models.name: Spiral multimodal alignment framework
models.genotype_or_key_features: integrates H&E, spatial transcriptomics, spatial proteomics on identical tissue sections

models.name: Omni‑TIS detection pipeline
models.genotype_or_key_features: detects and phenotypes tumor‑infiltrating stromal (TIS) niches from H&E, spatial RNA, and protein data

MOA_summary: Wang described deep‑learning pipelines that align H&E images with spatial transcriptomics and proteomics at single‑cell resolution, register serial sections into 3D tumor maps, and extract spatial niches (e.g., CAF subtypes, macrophage phenotypes, vascular structures, TIS) which can be quantified and linked to clinical outcomes such as immunotherapy response.
key_findings: Generated multimodal 3D maps of human tumors comprising >2 million cells, revealing spatial gradients of CXCL13 and CXCL5 signals.
Identified four distinct CAF spatial organizations that correlate with patient survival.
Discovered two macrophage phenotypes (TIM‑2⁺ immunosuppressive vs CXCL9⁺ immune‑active) with divergent spatial distributions across tumor cores and peripheries.
Developed Omni‑TIS framework that quantifies mature vs immature tumor‑infiltrating stromal niches and predicts response to checkpoint blockade in renal cell carcinoma.
Demonstrated that 3D reconstruction of TIS structures changes perceived volume and may improve biomarker robustness compared with 2D counts.
open_questions: How does 3D spatial architecture improve predictive power over traditional 2D metrics for immunotherapy response?
What is the optimal way to distill high‑dimensional spatial features into a single clinically actionable biomarker?
